transcript.overall: I cannot validate this plan as written because the plan itself was not delivered to me — every plan field arrived as `undefined`. My honest verdict on the plan-as-received: NOT EVALUABLE on PR-specific soundness; the council must re-emit the 15-PR body before Round 2 or all verdi…

pr_assessments.concern: The entire 15-PR plan body, masterplan summary, wave/dep overview, and carried risks were delivered to this seat as literal `undefined`. I have NO PR text. Any per-PR soundness/scope/sequencing verdict I emit on content I cannot see would be fabricated — which the charge forbids.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Council MUST re-emit the 15-PR plan body, the wave DAG, and the discovery findings before Round 2. Until then treat every content-specific verdict as unsupported. I have instead grounded the three named live-repo disputes against the actual repo so the replan can proceed on those.

pr_assessments.concern: Dispute #1 RESOLVED against building. The adaptive/feedback loop ALREADY terminates: axon/compiler/templates/feedback-loop.tpl.md bounds it with UNTIL(passed ≡ true OR iter ≥ max-iter), max-iterations default 3, ASSERT(max-iter>0), and a FAIL() non-convergence route. KERNEL-SLIM.md:155 also mechanically requires per-iteration termination guards on any cross-turn UNTIL/LOOP. A PR adding a termination guard fixes a solved problem and balloons the template.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Drop the termination-guard PR. If the underlying finding was about something narrower (e.g. the no-op `# Skip to next iteration` comment after an actor-FAIL in Phase 2, which does NOT actually continue), re-scope to that exact one-line defect and re-file with a test — do not add a whole termination subsystem.

pr_assessments.concern: Dispute #2 INVERTS. The dispute says promote/replay 'may already be discoverable.' Repo reality: they are not discoverable because they are not built as user features. 'promote' exists ONLY as scheduler starvation priority-promotion (scheduler/SCHEDULER.md:87, log/LOG.md:43); 'replay' appears in NO program, in COMMANDS.md, or in core/. So a 'surface these in the menu' PR has nothing to surface.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Verify-first: grep programs/ and COMMANDS.md for an actual promote/replay command. If absent (as I found), this is NOT a discoverability fix — re-file as a build-new PR for the promote/replay programs themselves, which then trips Core Rule 13 / R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST and the crucible gate (tests mandatory, human-runs-build).

pr_assessments.concern: Dispute #3 RESOLVED mostly against building. mode-menu EXISTS and is robust: COMMANDS.md:27-42 defines numeric shortcuts (1-7, D), EXEC(menu), and the mode badge. Full-menu render is ALREADY a hard kernel invariant (Core Rule 12, KERNEL-SLIM.md:73 — 'menu is ALWAYS rendered in full ... partial output is a shell crash'). ELSE branches render mechanically per OUTPUT-LAYER.md:35,90-100. The 'orphaned-ELSE breaks render' claim is contradicted by the spec.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Verify-first by actually booting the menu and feeding a program with a bare ELSE through the compiler. If render is correct (spec says it is), DROP. Only keep if a reproducible orphaned-ELSE render failure is captured — and then scope it to the compiler GRAMMAR.md ELSE rule, not a menu rewrite.

pr_assessments.concern: Any PR that assumes AXON rules HARD-block fights the live kernel posture. KERNEL-SLIM.md:89-94 ('Enforcement reality') states the rules are BLOCK-CAPABLE only when tools/hooks are installed via scripts/enable-enforcement.sh AND per-rule L:*-required flags are set; the DEFAULT is host-cap-enforce='self' with NO PreToolUse hook. 'surface-don't-enforce' is the actual contract.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Re-scope any enforcement PR to SURFACE state (narrate the verify.py status / R_STATE_SURFACED block) rather than to enforce hard blocks. Do not let a PR claim a rule 'cannot be bypassed' — the kernel explicitly warns against reading 'enforced' as 'cannot be bypassed.'

pr_assessments.concern: Any PR in this plan that ADDS a program or tool (e.g. a real promote/replay if PR-promote-replay is re-filed as build-new) must ship tests or it cannot be registered ACTIVE — Core Rule 13 / R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST (static BLOCK) + crucible gate (KERNEL-SLIM.md:73). This is the new-needs-tests constraint and it is one of the few hard-enforced rules.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Keep the constraint as a gating check across the whole plan: tag every PR that adds a neuron with a paired test PR in the same wave, and sequence the test before/with the registration. Human-runs-builds means the build/crucible step is operator-driven, so wave ordering must put the test-bearing change ahead of any ACTIVE registration.

pr_assessments.concern: PR-010 in the merged axon-tests project = tests/test_workspace_backup.py (structural test for workspace-backup.md) and the cron daemon activation (cron tick at boot, seed-defaults seeding 10 canonical entries). The charge labels PR-010 as 'reanchor cadence'. Reanchor in the live codebase refers to PR-CA-102 — the Copilot harness per-turn kernel-reload contract — and is already implemented and tested (test_copilot_instructions_sanity.py, test_harness_install.py, reanchor_store). 'Cadence' tuning of reanchor would touch workspace/harness/copilot.md and tools/harness_install.py. KNOW: reanchor is already wired and tested. pr_assessments.suggestion: Verify that reanchor_store and harness_install already cover the intended behavior before writing new code. Run tests/test_harness_install.py and tests/test_hooks.py to confirm 'reanchor' is already wired. If the intent is to change how frequently the per-turn reanchor fires, that is a kernel-discipline change (KERNEL-SLIM § COMPLIANCE ENFORCEMENT), not a new tool, and must go through the write-gate with L:dev-mode=true. Scope the PR to the specific cadence knob being changed and write a structural test that would fail if the knob regresses.

The charge labels PR-011 as 'audit-to-fix branch'. An 'audit-to-fix' pattern would involve axon_audit.py or tools/audit_axon_lang.py scanning for violations and emitting fix suggestions. KNOW: tools/audit_axon_lang.py exists and its test is test_audit_axon_lang.py. BELIEVE: the charge's 'audit-fix branch' framing describes a different feature, but the PR number collision creates traceability ambiguity. Assumption reversing verdict: the 15-PR plan uses a different project namespace where PR-011 is genuinely an audit-fix PR — but this was never disambiguated.
pr_assessments.suggestion: If the intent is to build an audit-then-fix pipeline on top of the existing audit_axon_lang.py or axon_audit.py, scope the PR to the specific finding catalog (axon/audit/) and the fix-applicator tool. Per CORE RULE 13, any new program added must have test coverage before it can be registered ACTIVE. The crucible gate must pass. Verify that tools/auto_audit.py + tools/auto_improve.py do not already cover the intended 'fix' step — auto-improve already has auto-compile, auto-tune, auto-archive actions with rollback. If the audit-fix branch is a subset of auto-improve's scope, this PR should be dropped or merged into auto-improve's backlog.

All three verification steps are deterministic and can be completed in under 5 minutes before the council replans.

transcript.overall: NOT EVALUABLE at the PR level (payload undefined), but the three disputes are now adjudicable as a verification gate against the live repo: all three resolve to verify-first because the program-layer artifacts they concern live under workspace/programs/, which is empty (0 files) …

pr_assessments.concern: Claim is that the adaptive loop may already self-terminate (so a termination-fix PR would be redundant). I cannot confirm or refute it: the adaptive-loop body lives in workspace/programs/ (orchestrator.md / mode-router), and workspace/ is EMPTY (0 files) in this checkout. The only loop bodies present are templates (axon/compiler/templates/feedback-loop.tpl.md) which DO terminate cleanly — UNTIL(passed OR iter>=max-iter) with a hard max-iterations default 3 and a FAIL() escape. If the live adaptive loop mirrors that template it already terminates and the PR is a no-op; if it is a hand-written orchestrator loop with no max-iter bound it does not. KNOW: template terminates. BELIEVE: live loop likely inherits the bound. Unverifiable here.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Before building: pull a checkout containing workspace/programs/orchestrator.md and grep its loop body for UNTIL/max-iter/converge. If a max-iteration bound + FAIL escape already exist (as in feedback-loop.tpl.md), DROP the termination PR as redundant. If absent, scope it to add ONLY the UNTIL bound + mid-loop identity re-assert (KERNEL G-02, line 154-162) — do not let it grow into a new loop state-machine.

pr_assessments.concern: Claim: promote/replay may already be discoverable, so a surfacing PR is redundant. Partial resolution from THIS repo: the CAPABILITY is real and mechanical — tools/loop_receipt.py INTENTS includes 'promote-ephemeral' and the 'recover' subcommand replays orphaned BEGUN->COMMITTED when live checksum==post.checksum (lines 25, 57, 362-366). So promote/replay is NOT fabricated. But 'discoverable' is a program/UI-tier property (menu, suggestions footer, code-dev replay command per WORKFLOW.md line 536), and that tier is in the empty workspace/. So whether a USER can find it is unverifiable here.
pr_assessments.suggestion: DROP any PR that re-implements promote/replay logic — it exists in loop_receipt.py. Scope the surviving work to discoverability ONLY: confirm whether menu.md / OUTPUT-LAYER suggestions footer / 'code-dev replay' already expose it (needs populated workspace/). If they do, drop; if not, a thin surface entry is kernel-aligned (surface-don't-enforce). Verify before building.

pr_assessments.concern: Claim: mode-menu has an orphaned-ELSE rendering bug. grep across axon/ and workspace/ found ZERO mode-menu / mode_menu files — the mode programs (mode-build/chat/dev/... per PROGRAMS-INDEX) and menu.md live in workspace/programs/, which is empty here. The kernel mandates full menu render (Core Rule 12, line 73: never truncate; violation == shell crash) and the active-program interrupt gate uses paired IF/ELSE blocks (lines 207-244) where a mis-indented ELSE would indeed strand output — so the bug CLASS is plausible against kernel structure. But the specific orphaned-ELSE cannot be located in this tree.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Do NOT build a render fix blind. Pull workspace/programs/menu.md + the mode-router and locate the actual orphaned ELSE; confirm it against Core Rule 12 (full-menu) and the OUTPUT-LAYER render path. If the ELSE is in a kernel gate (interrupt gate lines 207-244) it is a different, higher-severity fix than a workspace program. Verify-first; reorder ahead of any PR that depends on menu output being correct.

pr_assessments.concern: My role-specific charge asks whether PR-008 is bounded or balloons into a state-machine. I cannot see PR-008's text. pr_assessments.suggestion: When PR-008 text is supplied, check first whether it reuses existing state primitives (W:active-phase, loop-receipt ledger, _phases.json) vs. inventing new state. If it invents, SPLIT off the state-machine portion and require it to bind to existing tracking. Verify-first.

pr_assessments.concern: Any PR that ADDS a program or tool is kernel-compatible IF it ships tests — Core Rule 13 / R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST (KERNEL line 74) is a static BLOCK over the change-set plus the crucible gate, and existing untested neurons are grandfathered. This is the one PR class I can endorse structurally without the body, because the kernel constraint itself defines the acceptance bar.

transcript.overall: The plan-as-delivered remains non-evaluable in detail because the 15-PR payload is still undefined. However, the three workflow-engine PRs named in the charge (009, 010, 011) map to real AXON subsystems that can be grounded against the live repo. All three carry verify-first flag…

pr_assessments.concern: The adaptive-loop-may-already-terminate dispute is substantiated by live repo evidence. test_orchestrator_loop.py (L29-43) shows the orchestrator's decide() function already encodes three clean exit paths: fire, ask, and surface-only. The loop is bounded by conversation turns — there is no evidence of an infinite non-terminating path. If the finding that triggered PR-009 was 'the adaptive loop never exits', that finding is likely false against the live code. The PR may be fixing a non-bug. Assumption that would reverse this: the orchestrator.md source file itself (not found under workspace/programs/ in the repo scan) contains a LOOP(true) body with no UNTIL condition — this would re-confirm the bug. That file must be read before building.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Run: find the orchestrator.md source (CONTEXT.md references workspace/programs/orchestrator.md but the file was absent from the scanned programs directory — its absence is itself a signal). If it exists and has an unguarded LOOP(true), PR-009 is justified as a refine (add explicit UNTIL or max-iter guard). If decide() plus conversation-end is already the exit, drop PR-009 or narrow it to adding a max-iter safeguard as a defensive belt-and-suspenders measure scoped to workspace/ not axon/. New exit-condition logic requires a test per CORE RULE 13.

pr_assessments.concern: The promote/replay discoverability dispute is resolved by live repo: WORKFLOW.md §11 explicitly lists 'code-dev replay — Mine the project history for repeated lessons / expensive PRs' in the canonical command surface. The reanchor_store.py hook (tools/hooks/reanchor_store.py) is also real and ships already. If PR-010 aims to add discoverability for promote/replay, it is adding surface for commands that are already surfaced. The cadence aspect (when to reanchor) may be the legitimate finding — but it is distinct from discoverability. The PR title 'reanchor cadence' and the 'promote/replay already discoverable' dispute are two different claims that need to be separated before scoping the PR.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Split the verify-first into two sub-checks: (1) Run 'code-dev replay' and confirm what it surfaces — if the output is already rich and discoverable, the discoverability half of PR-010 is a non-fix. (2) Separately verify whether the reanchor cadence (when the orchestrator re-weights its signal set after promote events) is actually misconfigured. If cadence is the real bug, scope PR-010 narrowly to cadence configuration in workspace/preferences/ or workspace/programs/, not discoverability. Either sub-path requires a test covering the new cadence rule per R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST.

pr_assessments.concern: The audit→fix branch framing risks violating the surface-don't-enforce kernel constraint. AXON's design principle (WORKFLOW.md §6, KERNEL-SLIM CORE RULE 9, AGENTS.md hard rules) is explicit: AXON surfaces and specifies; the human implements and runs. An 'audit→fix branch' that automatically applies fixes would violate this. The live repo already has auto_improve.py (audit→auto-compile, auto-tune, auto-archive), but that is gated by L:auto-improve=false by default and is scoped to AXON's own workspace files — not user codebase. PR-011 must stay within the same boundary: surface findings, do not apply them. The CHANGELOG note that 113 programs exist with 86 nominal-missing primitives (from audit_axon_lang.py) suggests the audit surface is already functional; the 'fix branch' framing implies acting on those findings autonomously, which crosses the line.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Refine PR-011 to produce a surfaced fix-proposal output (a spec file or structured report in workspace/ listing each audit finding with a suggested remediation) rather than an auto-applied fix branch. The human or dev-mode session then applies the fixes. This keeps the PR within kernel constraints, is fully traceable, and does not require L:dev-mode bypass. If auto-application is genuinely intended for AXON's own internal files (not user codebase), gate it behind L:auto-improve and L:dev-mode=true, following the auto_improve.py pattern already in the repo. Add tests per CORE RULE 13 for the new audit-output format.

pr_assessments.concern: KERNEL-SLIM.md § COMPLIANCE ENFORCEMENT contains an explicit 'enforcement reality' block: gates are BLOCK-CAPABLE but bite mechanically ONLY when scripts/enable-enforcement.sh wires the Stop-hook AND per-rule L:*-required flags are set. A PR that hardens enforcement without landing the hook wiring will produce advisory-only rules that appear mechanical in the spec but are not. This violates the surface-don't-enforce kernel constraint as it currently stands.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Split into two sub-PRs: (a) hook wiring — scripts/enable-enforcement.sh + the PreToolUse/Stop hooks in tools/hooks/ — this is the prerequisite; (b) flag activation — L:*-required flags per rule, activated after hook is confirmed green. Sequence (a) before (b). Both need tests against the verify.py status surface, not just structural assertions.

pr_assessments.concern: CORE RULE 13 (R_NEW_NEEDS_TEST) is mechanically enforced by the crucible gate before merge. Any new program or tool added without test coverage is blocked at the gate. This is the strongest kernel constraint on new additions. If the PR scope (still undefined) adds programs, the tests are mandatory — not advisory.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Ensure each new program has both a structural test (in test_programs_md.py pattern) and a behavior fixture (synapse/sessions/ or equivalent). The crucible gate will block merge otherwise. No exception for 'small' programs — CORE RULE 13 grandfathers only existing untested neurons.

Wave sizing and critical path cannot be assessed without the plan payload.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Proposed sequencing from repo evidence: Wave 0 — verify the three disputes against live code (1-2 days); Wave 1 — mode-menu fix (if confirmed) + hook wiring sub-PR; Wave 2 — flag activation + new programs with tests; Wave 3 — any remaining UI fixes. Do not run Wave 2+ in parallel with hook wiring — the enforcement surface changes meaning once hooks are live.

pr_assessments.concern: The charge frames PR-010 as safety-critical because 'reanchor suppression would disarm advisory gates.' Verified in live repo: .claude/settings.json UserPromptSubmit hook runs reanchor_store.py || true, which always exits 0. However, reanchor_store.py only writes W:recent-user-input (prompt storage for anticipate/re-anchor feed) — it is NOT the identity reanchor gate itself. The kernel reanchor is a behavioral rule (KERNEL-SLIM.md § COMPLIANCE ENFORCEMENT) not dependent on this hook's exit code. The || true prevents a Python import error from blocking user prompts, which is correct for a non-blocking BG hook. Steelman: the naming 'reanchor' is misleading — this hook does prompt storage, not identity re-anchoring. Attack: if a future PR renames or repurposes this hook to carry actual gate logic, the || true becomes a real disarmament. Assumption that would reverse verdict: if PR-010 specifically upgrades this hook to carry gate logic without removing || true, the concern becomes critical. Must verify PR-010's actual diff scope before any verdict.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Require the PR-010 diff to be produced. Verify whether the hook's role is being expanded beyond prompt storage. If it remains pure prompt-store, the || true is correctly safe. If gate logic is added, || true must be removed and the hook must become blocking-capable. Add a comment in settings.json distinguishing advisory hooks (|| true acceptable) from gate hooks (must propagate exit codes).

pr_assessments.concern: In the council charge, PR-002 is flagged for 'changes enforcement labeling.' In the live repo, CHANGELOG PR-002 is the coverage gate (tools/rules/ 100%, tools/ 80%) — consistently labeled as CI enforcement in CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING.md, and the CI table (status: verified active). No mislabeling detected in the repo. However, the plan payload is undefined, so the PR-002 being evaluated in the council charge may be a DIFFERENT PR-002 from a separate plan (the AXON test-suite plan vs. the hr-team plan). The numbering collision is a real ambiguity. The coverage-gate PR-002 does not touch enforcement labels; if the council's PR-002 is something else entirely, this assessment is based on the wrong object.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Re-emit the charge with the actual hr-team plan payload to disambiguate PR-002 from the axon-tests PR-002 in CHANGELOG. Until then, any verdict on enforcement labeling is grounded in the wrong document. If the plan PR-002 does touch enforcement labeling in KERNEL-SLIM.md or OUTPUT-LAYER.md, require a before/after diff of the specific label strings (advisory vs. BLOCK-capable vs. enforced) and verify they match the actual hook posture confirmed in settings.json.

pr_assessments.concern: CHANGELOG PR-009 is test_r9_axon_write.py — 10 cases pinning the axon/ write-gate (R9). In the live repo, tools/hooks/enforce_pretooluse.py IS the mechanical implementation of R9: it calls enforce.py check-write and exits 2 to deny the tool call when dev-mode is off. This is the one real blocking gate in the system. PR-009's test coverage of R9 is directly security-relevant: without it, regressions in enforce.py could silently disarm the only mechanical write-gate. The test exists (tests/test_rules/test_r9_axon_write.py confirmed in repo). Steelman: keeping this test coverage is load-bearing for the security posture. Attack: the test is described as 'structural' — verify it exercises the actual enforce_pretooluse.py hook path, not just enforce.py in isolation, otherwise a hook wiring regression would not be caught.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Confirm test_r9_axon_write.py covers the full hook-to-enforce.py path (not just the enforce.py CLI in isolation). Add at least one integration test that exercises the PreToolUse hook simulation: mock a Write call targeting axon/ with dev-mode off and assert exit code 2. This closes the gap between unit-testing enforce.py and testing the hook wiring.

pr_assessments.concern: CHANGELOG PR-011 adds real test modules for r3, r7, r-coherence, r-drift-gate. These are advisory gates today (KERNEL-SLIM.md § Enforcement reality: 'bite mechanically ONLY when a host hook runs verify.py output every turn'). The verify_stop.py hook is LOG-ONLY (always exits 0), so these gates remain advisory until a pre-send hook is available. PR-011 adds tests for rules that are not yet mechanically enforced. This is not a security regression — it is groundwork. The risk is that tests passing against advisory-only rules create false confidence that gates are blocking. Steelman: test coverage before enforcement flip is the correct sequence (tests must exist before L:state-surfaced-required and similar flags are set to BLOCK). Attack: if PR-011 tests are written against advisory behavior and then the flags are flipped to BLOCK without updating tests to assert the harder guarantee, test suite may pass while real enforcement is broken.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Ensure PR-011 test cases are written in two tiers: (a) advisory-mode assertions (current), and (b) BLOCK-mode assertions gated behind a flag or environment variable. This way the test suite can be run in both modes and will fail loudly when the enforcement flip happens but the behavior does not match.

pr_assessments.concern: Live repo evidence: test_orchestrator_loop.py covers the per-tick ranker (synapse_suggest.rank) and the decide() branch logic across 5 seeded fixtures. It asserts both fixed and adaptive modes are exercised (T-111.5). However, there is NO test for loop termination: no test asserts that the orchestrator loop exits under any condition (goal achieved, context pressure critical, max-iterations reached, user abort). The CHANGELOG says the adaptive orchestrator loop is live (v1.1.4 entry). If the loop has no tested exit condition, an infinite-loop risk exists in production adaptive sessions — a security-relevant availability concern (context exhaustion, runaway token spend). This dispute is REAL and UNRESOLVED in the test suite.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Before any PR touching the orchestrator loop, add tests for: (1) loop exits when W:active-phase contains :done, (2) loop halts on context-pressure critical (>85% tokens), (3) loop exits after max-iterations guard, (4) user abort (answer=A) clears W:active-phase and routes new input. These are the four exit conditions implied by KERNEL-SLIM.md but untested.

pr_assessments.concern: Live repo evidence: tests/synapse/sessions/ FX-001 through FX-005 session fixtures exist, and test_orchestrator_loop.py test_replay_top1 and test_replay_decision exercise them. The replay path IS discoverable and testable. CHANGELOG PR-111 explicitly documents the replay harness. This dispute appears to be partially resolved — the promote/replay mechanism exists and has test coverage. The remaining gap is whether promote() (moving a candidate from suggestion to execution) is separately tested from replay(). The test suite exercises replay through rank() but may not exercise the promote path through the orchestrator's decision-to-EXEC transition.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Add one test that exercises the full promote path: fixture in adaptive mode, confidence >= threshold for 'fire' decision, assert EXEC is called on the top-1 candidate. This closes the promote vs. replay coverage gap.

pr_assessments.concern: Round 1 'kept' this, but the STUDY explicitly commits the deliverable as a GUIDELINE + asset pack, 'not a running program in this repo' (01-study.md executive summary). A PR that adds a new *program* may directly contradict the committed Q7a-pivot scope and the 'human-runs-builds' / text-only kernel constraints. 'Keep' was granted without anyone reading the PR or reconciling it against this scope decision.

pr_assessments.concern: 'Enforcement hardening' names a category that frontally collides with AXON's 'surface-don't-enforce' kernel constraint. Any PR that adds hard gates/enforcement rather than surfacing state is likely mis-framed at the title level, regardless of body. Round 1's 'refine' was the right instinct but unsupported.
pr_assessments.suggestion: Once authored, require this PR to express every check as a surfaced state signal plus a test (new-needs-tests), never a blocking enforcement. If it cannot be expressed as surfacing, it violates the kernel and should be dropped, not refined.
